defs: Any one of several species of small sandpipers, as the sanderling of Europe and America, the dunlin, the little stint of India (Tringa minuta), etc. Called also pume.

records.ety: [OE. stinten, stenten, stunten, to cause to cease, AS. styntan (in comp.) to blunt, dull, fr. stunt dull, stupid; akin to Icel. stytta to shorten, stuttr short, dial, Sw. stynta to shorten, stunt short. Cf. Stent, Stunt.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stinted; p. pr. & vb. n. Stinting.]

defs: To restrain within certain limits; to bound; to confine; to restrain; to restrict to a scant allowance.
quotes: I shall not go about to extenuate the latitude of the curse upon the earth, or stint it only to the production of weeds.
She stints them in their meals.

defs: To put an end to; to stop.
To assign a certain (i. e., limited) task to (a person), upon the performance of which one is excused from further labor for the day or for a certain time; to stent.
To serve successfully; to get with foal; -- said of mares.
quotes: The majority of maiden mares will become stinted while at work.
They can not stint till no thing be left.
And stint thou too, I pray thee.
The damsel stinted in her song.

records.ety: [Also written stent. See Stint, v. t.]

quotes: God has wrote upon no created thing the utmost stint of his power.

defs: Quantity or task assigned; proportion allotted.
quotes: His old stint -- three thousand pounds a year.
